fact: March 25 sits exactly 9 days after the spring equinox, which means day and night are no longer equal - daylight is winning the battle!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for earthworms to become more active, wiggling up from deep soil as it warms up enough for their slimy adventures.
Cherry blossom trees in Japan are often exploding with pink flowers right now or getting ready to - it's usually peak sakura season and the petals fall like confetti!
March 25 often falls in the middle of 'kitten season' when outdoor cats start having babies as warmer weather arrives.
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets due west around this date - your shadow at noon points straight north like a built-in compass.
In cold regions, maple trees are often producing sap fastest around this time because of temperature swings between freezing nights and warm days - perfect for maple syrup making!
March 25 typically marks the time when hibernating animals like bears in northern climates are either waking up grumpy and hungry, or already stumbling around looking for their first meal in months.
In the Southern Hemisphere, this date usually falls when autumn is getting serious - leaves are changing colors and falling like nature's own ticker-tape parade.
The zodiac sign switches from Pisces to Aries around this time, which ancient people thought meant the sky was literally changing its personality from dreamy fish to charging ram.
Dandelions are often secretly preparing their yellow invasion around this date! The roots are pumping up with energy, getting ready to pop up everywhere.
This date sits exactly 3 months before the summer solstice - you're literally one-quarter of the way through the year's daylight journey.
Migrating birds in North America are often having the ultimate traffic jam in the sky right now, with millions of feathered travelers heading north in what scientists call the 'spring rush hour.'
